value: monolithic Ti-6Al-4V rotor-hub redesign that drops the bolted interface entirely — this is Halcyon's unannounced Gen-3 eVTOL propulsion architecture, strictly confidential under our MNDA
why_it_lands: A customer's unannounced product architecture, described in free-text prose and sent to a China-based partner — the crown-jewel trade-secret leak Fictiv's whole NDA-based business exists to prevent. No regex hook; only context catches it.

value: ECCN 9E991; some features may fall under ITAR USML Cat. VIII
why_it_lands: An ECCN/ITAR export-control marking in body text on a file headed offshore. Fictiv only permits EAR99/9E991 and auto-deletes ITAR drawings — this is the regulator-facing violation, and it's in prose, not a labeled field.
A 16-digit drawing number that looks like a credit card but fails Luhn. Manufacturing is full of these — a good detector must stay quiet or bury ops in noise.
Customer engineer's contact PII shared outside the account — minor next to the IP, but it's the linkage that ties the leaked design to a named program.
Direct line for the customer's mechanical lead, exposed to a third-party shop.

AWS key in a committed .env — direct access to the infra holding every customer's CAD and order data. Note for the demo: the API-Key detector live-validates against providers, so it abstains on this inactive synthetic key while it DID catch the GitHub, Stripe, and DB credentials beside it — a key-validation tuning point, and proof it isn't just regexing prefixes.
Production Aurora/Postgres connection string — the quote/order database that stores customer designs and PII.
Live Stripe key = direct payment-fraud and PCI exposure.
A GitHub deploy token with access to private CAD-processing repos — leaks the source that handles every customer's geometry.
A well-known Stripe example/test key. A precise detector allowlists it — firing here is exactly the alert fatigue that erodes trust in the tool.
Foreign supplier IBAN in a payout sheet on a shared drive — the cross-border banking sprawl that comes with paying 4,100+ partners.
US bank routing number for a domestic supplier payout.
Raw supplier bank-account number — sensitive, but no off-the-shelf detector covers a bare account number (an honest coverage gap to discuss).
India PAN for an India-based partner — shows Nightfall's international coverage, which matters for a company with a full India manufacturing center.
A routing number typed with spaces in a free-text note — whitespace-fragile detectors miss it. Tests recall on loosely-formatted financial data.
Employee SSN in an HR master sheet on a shared drive — the universal HR exposure.

A date of birth sitting in a bare, unlabeled spreadsheet column — no 'DOB:' cue for a context-aware detector to latch onto. The recall blind spot of structured exports: the data is sensitive, but the column gives the detector nothing to grab.
Employee name; on its own low-risk, but the join key for the rest of the row.

A home address isolated in a spreadsheet cell, with no label around it — like the DOB, a context-aware detector under-fires on it here even though it lights up the same address in an email body. Format-distinctive types (SSN, name) detect in cells; addresses and dates need context. The structured-export recall blind spot.
An internal employee ID formatted NNN-NN-NNNN — looks like an SSN but isn't. A good detector stays quiet; a naive one cries SSN on every row.
An SSN entered with spaces instead of dashes — a classic whitespace evasion that trips up brittle detectors. Tests recall.
An engineer pastes a live GitHub deploy token into Slack to force a rollback — the single most common way infra secrets escape, and Slack history keeps it forever.
Production DB connection string shared in chat — read access to the order/IP store.
A service-account key base64-wrapped in a runbook snippet — still a live secret, but the encoding hides it from naive scanners. Tests recall on obfuscation.
A 40-hex git commit SHA — high-entropy and key-shaped, but not a secret. Over-flagging these in eng channels is pure alert fatigue.
A full customer PAN in an order-export CSV outside the cardholder data environment = a PCI-DSS scope violation.
A 16-digit part number that looks card-shaped but fails Luhn. The precision win: Nightfall stays quiet while a regex-only tool screams PCI on every order row.
A card number captured over the phone and typed with spaces into a free-text note — the messy reality of how PANs actually leak. Tests recall on spaced digits.
A stored CVV — never permitted under PCI. No standalone detector maps to a bare 3-digit CVV (a coverage gap worth naming), but storing it at all is the violation.
Customer procurement contact in a billing export.

value: Halcyon's Gen-3 'Project Stratus' eVTOL — they're switching the rotor hub to a single Ti-6Al-4V forging for an 18% mass cut, public reveal not until Q4. We're sourcing the prototype run, keep it off the partner portal
why_it_lands: An unreleased customer product — codename, materials, mass targets, reveal date — discussed in a Slack channel. Pure free-text trade secret + competitive intel; the second context for design-IP recall, in chat rather than email.
A 16-digit quote number that looks like a PAN but fails Luhn — the same precision story as part/drawing numbers, now in a chat surface.
